steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'est' (first occurrence) translates to?

hints.Imperfect: Imperfect 3rd sg. of 'esse' would be 'erat' — 'est' is present tense
hints.Perfect: Perfect 3rd sg. of 'esse' would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present tense
hints.Future: Future 3rd sg. of 'esse' would be 'erit' — 'est' is present tense (with 'futurus' forming a periphrastic future)

hints.eo, ire, ivi, itum: These are the principal parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are the principal parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.habeo, habere, habui, habitum: These are the principal parts of 'habere' (to have)

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'tremor' would be 'tremoris' — 'tremor' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'tremor' would be 'tremorem' — 'tremor' is the nominative subject
hints.Ablative: Ablative of 'tremor' would be 'tremore' — 'tremor' is the nominative form

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'tremoris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'tremoris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'tremoris' ends in -is

hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'iudex' would be 'iudicis' — 'iudex' is the nominative form
hints.Accusative: Accusative of 'iudex' would be 'iudicem' — 'iudex' is the nominative subject
hints.Vocative: Vocative of 'iudex' would be 'iudex' — but here it is the subject of 'est venturus'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'iudicis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'iudicis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'iudicis' ends in -is

hints.Perfect passive: Perfect passive would use a perfect passive participle — 'venturus' is a future active participle
hints.Present indicative: 'est' alone is present indicative, but 'est venturus' forms the active periphrastic (future active participle + esse)
hints.Passive periphrastic (must be come to): Passive periphrastic uses the gerundive — 'venturus' is a future active participle

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)
hints.vendo, vendere, vendidi, venditum: These are the principal parts of 'vendere' (to sell)
hints.verto, vertere, verti, versum: These are the principal parts of 'vertere' (to turn)

translation: How great the trembling will be when the judge is about to come
